file_name: XACCT Technologies, Inc.SUPPORT AND MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: XACCT will provide reasonable commercial efforts to provide the appropriate solutions for reported Incidents. Initial response times for reported Incidents are as set forth in Attachment 1.
perturbation.changed_text: XACCT will attempt to provide solutions for reported Incidents. Initial response times for reported Incidents are subject to XACCT's internal prioritization policies.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'reasonable commercial efforts' with 'attempt to provide' weakens the obligation. Stating response times are subject to 'internal prioritization policies' introduces ambiguity and allows XACCT to delay responses without clear justification.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's vague commitment to providing solutions and the prioritization of response times may constitute unfair business practices under California Business and Professions Code § 17200, which prohibits any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. By not adhering to reasonable commercial efforts to provide appropriate solutions, XACCT may be in violation of this code.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable commercial efforts' to 'attempt to provide' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity, as noted in the Perturbation Explanation. The addition that 'Initial response times are subject to XACCT's internal prioritization policies' further weakens the commitment. This could be argued as an 'unfair business act or practice' under California Business and Professions Code § 17200, as it allows for arbitrary delays in response times, potentially harming the client, therefore creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice,' which this change could violate.

perturbation.original_text: In order for Licensee to receive the Support Services referenced above, Licensee must: (a) Appoint Designated Support Contact(s), trained and qualified, who will maintain the integrity of the&sbsp;Product and who will act as Licensee's liaison for all technical communications with XACCT. The number of Designated Support Contact(s) will be determined by the level of Support Services purchased by Licensee set forth in Attachment 1referenced in Section 2. Names of Designated Support Contact(s) must be provided to XACCT prior to initial contact with the XACCT support center. All technical communications by Licensee to XACCT shall only be made by the Designated Support Contact(s). All information and materials provided to Licensee by XACCT pursuant to this Agreement will be routed to the Designated Support Contact(s). Licensee may change the Designated Support Contact(s) upon written notice to XACCT
perturbation.changed_text: In order for Licensee to receive the Support Services referenced above, Licensee should: (a) Endeavor to appoint Designated Support Contact(s), with general knowledge, who will attempt to maintain the integrity of the Product and act as a point of contact for technical communications with XACCT. The suggested number of contacts will be based on the level of Support Services purchased. Contact information should be provided to XACCT when convenient. Communications by Licensee to XACCT should generally be made by the Designated Support Contact(s). Information and materials provided to Licensee by XACCT may be routed to any employee. Licensee may change the Designated Support Contact(s) as needed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must' to 'should', 'trained and qualified' to 'general knowledge', 'maintain' to 'attempt to maintain', 'will be determined' to 'suggested', 'must be provided' to 'should be provided when convenient', 'shall only be made' to 'should generally be made', and 'will be routed' to 'may be routed' weakens and makes the entire section ambiguous and non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2204 dictates that a contract for sale of goods (which this support agreement relates to) can be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement. By making the obligations of the Licensee vague and non-mandatory, it introduces uncertainty as to whether there was ever actually a 'meeting of the minds' which renders the agreement potentially unenforceable in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2204 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract [2201 - 2210] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2204. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract by changing mandatory obligations to discretionary ones. This introduces ambiguity, potentially undermining the 'agreement' required by California Commercial Code § 2204. The original text used 'must,' indicating a clear requirement, whereas the changed text uses 'should,' making the obligation non-mandatory. Similarly, changing 'will be determined' to 'suggested' and 'shall only be made' to 'should generally be made' dilutes the contractual certainty. According to snippet 1, COM 2204 (1), 'A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement'. The shift to ambiguous language in the changed text introduces uncertainty regarding whether there was ever actually a 'meeting of the minds', thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: XACCT will use reasonable commercial efforts to provide maintenance releases and Minor Releases to the then-current embodiment of the Product that it provides to its customers generally. Maintenance Releases and Minor Releases may also include one copy of revisions to the documentation applicable to such maintenance releases and Minor Releases.
perturbation.changed_text: XACCT may, at its discretion, provide maintenance releases and Minor Releases. Maintenance Releases and Minor Releases may or may not include revisions to the documentation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will use reasonable commercial efforts' to 'may, at its discretion' makes the provision of maintenance releases entirely optional for XACCT. Also, changing 'may also include one copy of revisions to the documentation applicable to such maintenance releases and Minor Releases' to 'may or may not include revisions to the documentation' gives XACCT complete discretion over providing updated documentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1655 states that if the parties intend something in a contract but do not make an explicit provision, a reasonable stipulation is implied. By removing the requirement to use 'reasonable commercial efforts' and making updates optional, the ambiguity could allow XACCT to avoid providing necessary updates, potentially contradicting the implied reasonable stipulation that the software will be maintained to a usable standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1655. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The change from "will use reasonable commercial efforts" to "may, at its discretion" directly contradicts the principle outlined in California Civil Code § 1655, which states that stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable are implied when the contract doesn't explicitly address them. By making maintenance releases optional, the changed text removes an implied reasonable stipulation for maintaining the software to a usable standard. The original contract suggested an obligation to provide maintenance; the changed text negates this, creating ambiguity and a potential for non-compliance with implied reasonable standards as understood in law.
